Introduction :
Google Sign-In is one of the easiest ways to add secure authentication to your web application. Instead of building a complete login system from scratch, you can let users sign in with their Google account in just a few clicks.
Before you can implement Google Sign-In, you’ll need to create Google OAuth 2.0 credentials. In this guide, we’ll walk through the complete setup process using Google Cloud Console.
What Are Google OAuth Credentials?
When a user signs in with Google, your application sends an authentication request to Google’s servers.
To identify your application, Google requires two credentials:

• Client ID: A public identifier that tells Google which application is requesting authentication.

• Client Secret: A private credential that your backend uses to securely verify your application’s identity. Never expose this value in frontend code.
These credentials help Google verify which application is requesting authentication. Since millions of applications use Google Sign-In, this process ensures that authentication requests come from legitimate sources.
If you’ve already created your OAuth credentials, you can skip directly to integrating them into your application.
Step 0 :

• Before you begin, ensure that your Google account has Two-Step Verification (2FA) enabled. Google may require this before allowing you to create OAuth credentials.
Step 1 : Open “Google Cloud Console”
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Google Cloud ConsoleStep 2 : Create a New Project

• After the console opens, click “Select a project” in the top left corner.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Select a project Button
• A dialog box will appear. Click “New project” in the top-right corner of the dialog box to create a new project.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

New project
• Now enter a “Project name” and select a “Organization” if you have any otherwise just click “Create”.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

New Project Create Page
• Once created, click again on the “Select a project” button it will show the project which you just created click on that project and it will redirect to the dashboard of the created project.
Step 3 : Configure the OAuth Consent Screen

• Before creating OAuth credentials, Google requires some basic information about your application.

• From the left sidebar : API & Services > OAuth Consent Screen

• Then click on “Get Started” if prompted.

• After that fill the information asked first one by one. ( You can just follow the images below )
Press enter or click to view image in full size

• Choose External if your app is available to any Google user.

• Choose Internal if you’re creating an application only for users within your organization.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Press enter or click to view image in full size

• Click on “Finish” and then “Continue”.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Step 4 : Create OAuth Credentials

• Click on “Create OAuth client” as shown in the image below.

• Select “Application Type” and give a meaningful name to the app.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Step 5 : Configure Authorized JavaScript Origins

• Add every domain from which your frontend will make authentication requests.

• For local development:
http://localhost:3000
• If you’re using Vite:
http://localhost:5173
• If your application is deployed:
https://yourdomain.com
• Replace yourdomain.com with your actual domain.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Step 6: Configure Authorized Redirect URIs

• Next, add the redirect URLs where Google should send users after successful authentication and then click “Create”.

• Examples:
http://localhost:3000/auth/google/callbackor
http://localhost:5000/auth/google/callback
• Your redirect URI must exactly match the URI configured in your backend.

• Even a missing slash can cause authentication to fail.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Step 7: Create the Credentials

• Click “Create ” ( which is in above image ).

• Google will immediately generate your:“ Client ID & Client Secret”

• A popup similar to the following will appear.

• copy the credentials and sometimes you may not see Client Secret here for that you can follow the last step.
Final Step : Getting Credentials

• If you ever forget your credentials or need to access them again, you can retrieve them from the Clients section in Google Cloud Console.

• Simply click the Edit (pencil) icon next to your OAuth client. This will open a page similar to the one shown below, where you can view your Client ID and Client Secret.

• You can return to this page at any time to copy your credentials whenever you need them in the future.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Common Errors
Error 1: redirect_uri_mismatch
This usually means the redirect URI in your application doesn’t exactly match the one configured in Google Cloud Console.
Double-check:

• Protocol (http vs https)

• Port number

• Path

• Trailing slash
Error 2: Access blocked
This usually occurs when:

• OAuth Consent Screen isn’t configured.

• You’re using testing mode without adding yourself as a test user.
Error 3: invalid_client
This often happens because:

• Client ID is incorrect.

• Client Secret is incorrect.

• Environment variables aren’t loaded properly.
Security Best Practices
Always follow these guidelines:

• Never commit your Client Secret to GitHub.

• Store credentials in environment variables.

• Restrict OAuth credentials to authorized domains.

• Rotate credentials if they are ever exposed.

• Keep production and development credentials separate.
Final Thoughts :
Setting up Google OAuth may seem overwhelming at first, but once you’ve completed it, integrating Google Sign-In into your application becomes much easier.
With your “Client ID” and “Client Secret” ready, you’re now prepared to implement secure Google authentication in your application using your preferred backend framework.
I hope this guide helped simplify the setup process. Happy coding!
If this guide helped you, consider giving it a clap and following me for more web development and AI integration tutorials.
